source_alignment.subject: Mathematics
source_alignment.source_statement: Interpret and construct pie charts and line graphs and use these to solve problems; calculate and interpret the mean as an average.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack connects data representation with reasoning: choose what the graph shows, read scales accurately and interpret mean as a fair-share average.

objective.child_goal: I can read graphs carefully and explain what the data shows.
objective.statement: Interpret line graphs and pie charts, solve comparison problems from data and calculate the mean as a fair-share average.
misconceptions: Reads graph intervals as one unit without checking the scale.
Treats a pie chart sector as an exact amount without using the total.
Calculates the mean but cannot interpret it as an average or fair share.
Makes a conclusion from a graph without checking labels, units or context.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The amount each step on an axis or chart represents.
vocabulary.audio_script: The scale tells us what each interval is worth.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Axis tick marks lighting up in equal jumps.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A graph that shows how data changes, often over time.
vocabulary.audio_script: A line graph helps us see change between points.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Data points connected across a time axis.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A circle split into sectors to show parts of a whole.
vocabulary.audio_script: A pie chart shows how a whole amount is shared into parts.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A circle divided into labelled slices.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An average found by sharing the total equally.
vocabulary.audio_script: The mean is the fair-share average.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Unequal stacks levelled to the same height.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Read the data and explain what it means in the context.
vocabulary.audio_script: To interpret data is to explain the story it tells.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A graph connected to a short conclusion card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The axis labels go 0, 20, 40, 60. What is each interval worth?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Retrieve scale reading before interpreting data points.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Before reading a graph, check the value of each interval.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Axis ticks pulse in jumps of twenty.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Graphs are clues. Check the title, labels, scale and units before you decide what they show.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A data detective reads the whole graph, not just the biggest shape.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A checklist lens scans title, labels, scale and data.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Use the line graph to find how much the temperature increased from 10:00 to 14:00.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model reading two points and finding the difference using units.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Read both values from the scale, then subtract to find the increase.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two graph points drop guide lines to labelled axes before a difference bar appears.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: If 1/4 of 120 votes chose blue, how many votes is that?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect pie chart sectors to fractions of a total.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A sector is a part of the whole. Use the total to find the amount.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A quarter sector separates from a 120-vote circle and becomes 30 counters.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Find the mean of 6, 8, 10 and 12 using fair sharing.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Diagnose whether the mean is understood as equal sharing, not just a procedure.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Add the values, then share the total equally between the four data points.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Four stacks level out to height nine.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why we must check the scale before saying the line went up by 3.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The line moved three intervals, but each interval might be worth more than one.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion counts intervals first, then reads the scale value.

manipulatives.purpose: Give learners a consistent routine for reading statistical displays before solving problems.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners scan title, labels, scale and units, trace graph points, compare values and approve or reject data claims.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Provide sonified point values, keyboard point navigation, high-contrast optional gridlines, text descriptions of charts and a reduced-data mode.

manipulatives.purpose: Make the mean visible as equal sharing of a total rather than a memorised formula.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners move counters between stacks, then connect the fair-share model to total divided by number of values.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: No-drag tap controls, numeric labels on stacks, audio count summaries and static before-after panels for reduced motion.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave line graphs, pie charts and mean only after learners reliably check scale, labels and total; use reasoning prompts for claims drawn from data.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Read exact and interval values from line graphs with varied scales.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: line graph metadata + target point or difference + scale-aware choices
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use clear units and avoid visual clutter; include non-unit scales from expected band onward.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Convert pie chart sectors into quantities using the stated total.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: total + sector fraction/percentage + question about count or comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Check percentage and fraction values are Year 6 appropriate and exact where required.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Calculate and interpret the mean as an equal-share average.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: 3 to 6 data values + fair-share animation + interpretation question
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include contexts where mean is useful and contexts where it may hide variation in secure band.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Judge whether a statement is supported by the data display.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: graph or chart + claim + support/refute/not enough information choices
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review needed to keep claims fair and avoid misleading data literacy traps.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain scale reading, pie chart total use and mean interpretation through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review scheduler selects one short data display or fair-share item by weakest signal
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval displays small and accessible on mobile screens.

body.prompt: A line graph shows temperature was 12 degrees C at 10:00 and 18 degrees C at 14:00. How much did the temperature increase?

expected_answer.value: 6 degrees C

hints: Read the two values first.
Subtract the earlier value from the later value.
question_variants.explanation: The increase is 18 - 12 = 6 degrees C.

body.prompt: A vertical axis is labelled 0, 25, 50, 75, 100. A point is halfway between 50 and 75. What value does it show?

hints: The gap from 50 to 75 is 25.
Half of 25 is 12.5.
question_variants.explanation: Halfway between 50 and 75 is 50 + 12.5 = 62.5.

body.prompt: A pie chart shows 1/4 of 120 votes were for blue. How many votes were for blue?

hints: Find one quarter of the total.
120 divided by 4 equals 30.
question_variants.explanation: One quarter of 120 is 120 / 4 = 30, so 30 votes were for blue.

body.prompt: Find the mean of 6, 8, 10 and 12.

hints: Add the four values.
Share the total equally between four values.
question_variants.explanation: 6 + 8 + 10 + 12 = 36. Sharing 36 equally between 4 values gives a mean of 9.

body.prompt: A chart shows 18 pupils chose football, 12 chose dance and 10 chose swimming. Which claim is supported?
choices: Football was chosen by 6 more pupils than dance.
Swimming was chosen by half the class.
Dance was the most popular activity.

expected_answer.value: Football was chosen by 6 more pupils than dance.

hints: Compare football and dance.
question_variants.explanation: The data shows 18 for football and 12 for dance, so football had 6 more pupils.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Reads graph intervals as one unit without checking the scale.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner counts grid spaces instead of using axis labels.
repair_sequence: Cover the data and read only the labelled axis values.
Find the value of one interval.
Return to the data point and trace to the correct value.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner reads a new non-unit scale accurately and explains the interval value.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats a pie chart sector as an exact amount without using the total.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says a quarter means 25 without checking whether the total is 100.
repair_sequence: Label the whole circle with the total.
Split the whole into equal sector parts.
Calculate the matching share of the total.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner finds a sector count from a new total and states the fraction-of-total relationship.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Calculates the mean but cannot interpret it as an average or fair share.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner follows add-divide steps but cannot explain what the result represents.
repair_sequence: Build the values as stacks of counters.
Move counters until all stacks are equal.
Connect the balanced height to total divided by number of values.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner calculates a mean and explains it as the value each group would have if shared equally.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Makes a conclusion from a graph without checking labels, units or context.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses a claim based only on the tallest bar or steepest line.
repair_sequence: Use the data detective checklist: title, labels, scale, units.
Highlight the exact data needed for the claim.
Classify the claim as supported, refuted or not shown.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner rejects an unsupported data claim and names the missing or contradictory evidence.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm grid highlighting, no flashing chart transitions and supportive feedback for data-reading errors.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace moving point traces, pie slices and balancing stacks with static labelled panels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read chart titles, labels and units before the question; allow replay of each data value.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use line focus, simplified display captions and chunked claim text.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Guide learners through one checklist item at a time before revealing answer choices.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow untimed graph inspection, persistent axes and optional worked examples for scale intervals.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit correct scale checking and data selection even when arithmetic needs repair.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach scale, axis, sector, total, mean, average, increase, decrease and supported claim with visual anchors.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires accurate scale reading, simple line graph and pie chart problems and mean calculation; secure requires interpreting mean and judging whether claims are supported by chart evidence.
evidence.parent_summary: Can read graphs and pie charts carefully, calculate averages and explain what data shows.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Checks labels, scales and units; solves line graph and pie chart problems; interprets mean as fair share; critiques claims from data.
next_actions: Interleave statistics with reasoning packs using supported/refuted claim prompts.
Route repeated scale errors to graph-reader repair before complex graph comparisons.
Use real-classroom-style but anonymised contexts with no personal data collection.

qa.notes: Year 6 statistics pilot reaches 240 variants with five curated questions preserved semantically unchanged and 235 deterministic review candidates. All five declared interaction formats are used across non-unit line-graph scales, justified and unjustified interpolation, comparison/difference/change problems, exact pie fractions/percentages/angles/totals, means of three to six values, missing-value reasoning, fair-share interpretation, data-claim critique, representation choice and transfer. Generated items provide graph-table/text equivalents, keyboard/no-drag routes, high contrast, reduced-data and static reduced-motion alternatives with pressure-free data-detective missions. Selected graph narration references are produced, human-reviewed ElevenLabs sonified point summaries only; browser TTS is prohibited. Independent mathematics, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
